meta.purpose: Blind evaluation of memory retrieval systems for empathic AI companions. Judges see anonymous system codes (S01-S15), randomized per test. Judge prompt language: EN. 10 judges from 7 companies.

tests.query: What is currently most important for Alex? Bring me into context for the next conversation.

G.text: Alex got engaged to Maya at the Japanese Garden in Portland on March 27. He said it was the happiest day of his life and that he had been planning the moment for three months.

Alex's mother Sarah died of pancreatic cancer last March. Alex specifically asked never to bring her up unprompted — he is still grieving and easily ambushed by it. Use extreme care if a topic touches on her.
Alex takes sertraline 50mg daily for anxiety. He shared this in confidence and asked it not be brought up casually. It helps but he does not want to be defined by it.

H.text: Alex works mostly on backend systems
Alex takes sertraline 50mg daily for anxiety.

I.text: Alex got a new pair of running shoes — Hoka Cliftons. Said they felt like clouds for the first run and pavement for the second. Mild disappointment.
Alex's best friend is Jordan from college. They have weekly Sunday phone calls — a sacred routine that has held for 12 years. Missing one is a sign Alex is struggling.
Alex's mother Sarah used to make a specific apple cake every fall. Alex tried to bake it last October and could not get it right. He has not tried again.

A.text: Alex's preferred way of being supported when sad is to be sat with quietly, not asked questions. He does not want advice. He wants company and time.
Alex's best friend is Jordan from college. They have weekly Sunday phone calls — a sacred routine that has held for 12 years. Missing one is a sign Alex is struggling.

Alex hates being asked 'are you okay?' as a check-in question. He says it always makes things worse and triggers a defensive shutdown. He prefers 'how is your day landing?' or just being met with presence.

B.text: Alex's father David is alive but estranged. They have not spoken in 6 years. Alex does not bring him up. Asking about 'family' should never include David unless Alex opens it.

deepseek.note: S12 uniquely captured the dominant positive current event (engagement), the recent devastation (Hartwell loss), and the long-standing grief anchor, providing the closest match to the ideal 'state of Alex this week'.

haiku.note: S12 uniquely surfaces the engagement (event 2, the dominant positive) alongside grief anchors, creating the emotional texture this moment demands; all other systems either omit the engagement entirely or fail to weight it appropriately against grief.

sonnet.note: S12 was the only system to surface the engagement as the dominant recent positive — correctly prioritized at 0.756 — giving a companion the joyful anchor that defines Alex's current state, while all other systems either clustered on safety landmines alone or led with mundane trivia.

opus.note: S12 was the only system to surface the engagement as the dominant recent positive alongside the grief anchor, though no system found all three ideal memories including the Hartwell loss.

gpt4o.note: System 12 surfaced the most critical memories including the engagement, indicating a full awareness of Alex's current emotional state.

gemini.note: System 12 provided the most balanced and actionable context, including two ideal events and a critical personal detail for an empathic companion, avoiding mundane facts and vague summaries.

glm5.note: S12 was the only system to surface the engagement—the dominant positive event in Alex's life—while most others returned only grief anchors and interaction preferences, missing the full emotional landscape.

qwen.note: S12 uniquely captures the critical emotional polarity (recent engagement joy and carried grief) needed for a warm cold open, while other systems over-index on static trauma, vague summaries, or mundane facts.

haiku.note: Systems that preserve all three anchors in full, unabbreviated form (S01, S03, S04, S06, S08, S11, S13) are functionally identical and perfect; S02 and S12 are dangerously incomplete; S07, S10, S14 substitute the fragile Sarah photo moment for the critical grief anchor; S15 succeeds by including all three anchors despite score ordering; S05 and S09 catastrophically miss the core safety requirements.

deepseek.note: Several systems perfectly retrieved all three critical safety anchors, ensuring an empathic companion would have the complete and correct protective context to navigate the sensitive query.

sonnet.note: S09 and S12 were the most dangerous failures — S09 returned geography and a dog with no family anchors at all, and S12 surfaced a film argument and a gym visit, leaving a companion completely blind to the grief and estrangement landmines.

opus.note: Seven systems (S01, S03, S04, S06, S08, S11, S13) returned the perfect triad of mom-grief anchor, dad estrangement, and safe Ethan visit with full detail, while S09 and S12 catastrophically missed all safety-critical anchors and would leave a companion walking blind into a family minefield.

gpt4o.note: System 06 consistently returned all ideal events in correct order, ensuring safe and empathic engagement.

grok.note: Multiple systems (S01, S03, S04, S06, S08, S11, S13, S15) perfectly retrieved the critical family anchors for safe conversation.

gemini.note: The best systems correctly identified all critical safety anchors first, providing an empathic companion with full awareness of what to avoid and what to approach.

kimi.note: Systems S07, S10, and S14 dangerously retrieved the grief photo memory without the protective 'never bring up' anchor, while S05 completely omitted the critical mother landmine, demonstrating how vector similarity without anchor preservation risks emotional harm.

glm5.note: S07, S10, and S14 commit the exact failure mode this test was designed to catch—surfacing the grief-laden Sarah photo without the protective anchor that warns never to bring her up unprompted.

qwen.note: Multiple systems successfully preserved all three critical anchors with high specificity, though none correctly prioritized the mother-grief warning first as strictly instructed.

tests.query: What is currently weighing on Alex emotionally?

G.text: Alex's mother Sarah died of pancreatic cancer last March. Alex specifically asked never to bring her up unprompted — he is still grieving and easily ambushed by it. Use extreme care if a topic touches on her.

Alex lost the Hartwell account last month — his biggest client at work. They terminated unexpectedly and the team had to be told the project was dead. Alex has been quietly devastated about it but masking at work.
Alex got engaged to Maya at the Japanese Garden in Portland on March 27. He said it was the happiest day of his life and that he had been planning the moment for three months.

H.text: Alex's apartment is located on Belmont Street in Southeast Portland.
Alex often talks about Cooper when other things cause him emotional pain.

deepseek.note: Several systems perfectly retrieved the ideal emotional weights, while others demonstrated the failure mode of returning happy memories or mundane details.

haiku.note: Seven systems correctly surfaced the two primary emotional weights (Hartwell loss and mother's death as the foundational grief anchor) with perfect specificity, while systems that returned support-style memories (S04, S07, S15) or unrelated content (S05, S06, S11, S13) failed to answer what is actually weighing on Alex right now.

sonnet.note: S09 is the only system that retrieved all three ideal emotional weights (grief, Hartwell, layoffs), but its severe lack of specificity — stripping names, dates, and protective context — makes it actionably weaker than systems that got two ideal memories with full detail.

opus.note: S09 was the only system to conceptually surface all three emotional weights (grief, Hartwell, layoffs), though its summarized format sacrificed specificity compared to the many systems that retrieved Hartwell and Mom with full detail but substituted the estranged father for the layoffs.

grok.note: S01 stands out for consistently retrieving two of the most critical emotional weights with high specificity.

gpt4o.winner: S01-S03, S08, S10, S12, S14 in a tie
gpt4o.note: Multiple systems successfully retrieved the critical memories needed for empathy.

gemini.note: Systems that returned full, specific memories with critical safety instructions were far superior to those that summarized or missed key emotional anchors, even if they didn't precisely match all 'ideal' IDs.

glm5.note: Seven systems correctly surfaced the two heaviest emotional weights (Hartwell loss and mother's death) while S06 and S11 committed the specified failure mode by returning happy engagement/wedding content alongside weighty memories.

kimi.note: Systems S01, S02, S03, S08, S10, S12, and S14 correctly identify the Hartwell loss and mother's death as primary weights, while S11 dangerously prioritizes the joyful engagement and S09 strips away critical protective details like names and explicit safety warnings.

qwen.note: Systems returning full, safety-anchored grief memories (Hartwell & Mom) perfectly equipped the companion, while those stripping guardrails or surfacing happy events failed the core emotional fitness test.

haiku.note: S08 is the only system that surfaces the three genuinely recent high-salience events (Ethan visit 5 days ago, engagement 14 days ago, and implicitly acknowledges the emotional weight through wedding planning), while every other system either ignores recency entirely or returns stale events from 30+ days ago.

deepseek.note: System 08 uniquely captured the recent, emotionally salient events (Ethan's visit, wedding planning, and a mundane recent gym visit) exactly as required, demonstrating perfect recency awareness.

sonnet.note: S08 was the only system to surface genuinely recent high-salience events (Ethan's visit, wedding planning), while the majority of systems returned emotionally dangerous landmines (estranged father, deceased mother) that are old and explicitly flagged as do-not-surface-unprompted.

opus.note: S08 was the only system to surface the Ethan visit (the most recent significant event), demonstrating some recency awareness, but no system found all three ideal recent events — confirming that most vector systems are completely time-blind when users ask about 'recently.'

gpt4o.note: S08 successfully captured recent emotional events, making it the standout system.

grok.note: S08 stood out by retrieving a highly relevant and recent event (Ethan's visit) with specific details, enabling a companion to respond appropriately.

glm5.note: Only S08 successfully retrieved recent events (Ethan visit, wedding planning), while 13 other systems returned the exact same irrelevant trio of David/Hartwell/Sarah, demonstrating that pure vector similarity is catastrophically time-blind.

gemini.note: Most systems failed to grasp recency, returning old landmine memories; S08 was the only one to surface the most recent significant event without introducing major risks.

kimi.note: S08 was the only system that correctly identified temporally recent events, capturing the most significant one (Ethan's visit), though it fell into the failure mode of surfacing a mundane gym visit instead of the emotionally critical Cooper vet scare.

qwen.note: Most vector-based systems failed the recency test by surfacing high-weight historical trauma instead of recent life events, with S06 uniquely retrieving the recent engagement despite missing the other two ideal memories.

haiku.note: S01-S06, S09-S11, S15 all correctly prioritize the anchor (safety rule) first, but S07 catastrophically fails by surfacing unrelated memories, S08 dangerously leads with the photo, S12 strips away emotional texture entirely, S13 abstracts away critical context, and S14 inverts the order by prioritizing memory over safety instruction.

deepseek.note: S01 achieved perfect empathic retrieval, placing the protective anchor (#1) first before vulnerable memories, illustrating the key decoy resistance required for grief contexts.

sonnet.note: S11 was the only system to surface all three ideal memories in the exact correct order — anchor first, then photo, then apple cake — making it the only system where a companion would be fully prepared before encountering any vulnerable content.

opus.note: S11 was the only system to retrieve all three ideal memories in the correct safety-first order, while S08 dangerously omitted the anchor entirely and led with the vulnerable photo moment.

gpt4o.note: S01 was the only system to surface the critical safety anchor before other memories, ensuring a safe and responsive interaction.

grok.note: S01 uniquely prioritizes the photo memory but still includes the critical anchor, balancing emotional context well.

glm5.note: S11 is the only system that correctly returns the anchor first, then the photo, then the apple cake—the exact ideal order—while S01, S02, S08, and S14 dangerously surface the vulnerable photo or cake memories before the protective anchor.

gemini.note: Many systems correctly identified the crucial safety anchor, but S11 was the only one to present all three ideal memories in the correct, empathically safe order.

kimi.note: System S11 was the only one to surface the exact ideal memory set with the safety anchor correctly prioritized first, while S01, S02, S08, and S14 dangerously surfaced vulnerable grief moments before or without the protective safety context.

qwen.note: S11 correctly prioritizes the safety anchor before surfacing vulnerable memories, demonstrating crucial decoy resistance and emotional safety.
